prerequisites.question: What does the expression (x + 3)(x - 2) simplify to?
prerequisites.remediation: Use FOIL: multiply First (x·x), Outer (x·-2), Inner (3·x), Last (3·-2), then combine like terms.

prerequisites.question: If f(x) = 3x - 4, what is f(2)?
prerequisites.remediation: Function notation f(2) means substitute x = 2: f(2) = 3(2) - 4 = 6 - 4 = 2.

prerequisites.question: What is the y-intercept of the line y = 2x - 5?
prerequisites.remediation: The y-intercept is where x = 0. Substitute 0 for x: y = 2(0) - 5 = -5, so the point is (0, -5).

beats.title: Tossing a Ball
beats.goal: Build intuition that a quadratic models a ball's path and has a single highest point (the vertex).

skeleton.text: Imagine tossing a ball. Its path through the air is a curve called a parabola — the graph of a quadratic function. Watch it fly.
Notice how it rises, peaks, then falls. That single peak — the highest point — is called the vertex.
Now try the angle slider. Before you drag: what happens to the peak height as you steepen the launch angle toward straight up?

check.text: As you increase the launch angle toward vertical, the ball's maximum height...

beats.title: Shape of a Parabola
beats.goal: Show that changing 'a' in y = ax^2 controls the width and direction of the parabola.

skeleton.text: The simplest quadratic is y = x^2. The coefficient 'a' in front of x^2 changes the shape.
When a = 2, the parabola gets steeper — it 'hugs' the y-axis.
When a = 0.3, it flattens out — wider and shallower.
And when a is negative — here a = -1 — the parabola flips upside down. Its vertex is now a highest point instead of a lowest one.

beats.title: The Vertex
beats.goal: Teach that every parabola y = ax^2 + bx + c has a vertex at x = -b/(2a), and show how to find its y-value.

skeleton.text: Here's y = 2x^2 - 8x + 5. Every parabola has exactly one vertex — its turning point.
The x-coordinate of the vertex is always x = -b/(2a). Here a = 2 and b = -8, so x = {{vertexX(2,-8)}}.
Plug that x back into the function to get y = {{vertexY(2,-8,5)}}. So the vertex is ({{vertexX(2,-8)}}, {{vertexY(2,-8,5)}}).
The vertical line through the vertex — x = {{vertexX(2,-8)}} — is the axis of symmetry. The parabola is a mirror image across this line.
Drag this orange point along the curve. Notice the y-value is lowest exactly at the vertex — that's why it's the minimum.

beats.title: Roots and the Discriminant
beats.goal: Show that roots are x-intercepts and the discriminant b^2 - 4ac tells how many there are.

skeleton.text: This is y = x^2 - 2x - 3. Where does it cross the x-axis? Those points are called roots or zeros.
The roots are x = {{root1(1,-2,-3)}} and x = {{root2(1,-2,-3)}}. The discriminant D = b^2 - 4ac = {{disc(1,-2,-3)}} is positive, so there are two real roots.
Now look at y = x^2 - 4x + 4. D = {{disc(1,-4,4)}} — a discriminant of zero means exactly one root, where the parabola just touches the x-axis.
And y = x^2 + 2 has D = {{disc(1,0,2)}} — a negative discriminant. The parabola floats above the x-axis; there are no real roots.

check.text: If a quadratic has discriminant D = 0, how many x-intercepts does its graph have?

beats.title: Factored Form
beats.goal: Connect roots to factored form y = a(x - r)(x - s) and show how to find roots by factoring.

skeleton.text: The quadratic y = x^2 - x - 6 can be rewritten as y = (x + 2)(x - 3). This is the factored form.
Set each factor to zero: x + 2 = 0 gives x = {{root1(1,-1,-6)}}; x - 3 = 0 gives x = {{root2(1,-1,-6)}}. These roots are exactly where the graph crosses the x-axis.
The factored form y = a(x - r)(x - s) hands you the roots r and s immediately. The vertex sits halfway between them at x = {{vertexX(1,-1)}}.
Three forms, one function. Each reveals different features. Can you factor x^2 - 5x + 6 in your head?

beats.title: Completing the Square
beats.goal: Derive vertex form by completing the square and read the vertex (h, k) directly.

skeleton.text: Start with y = x^2 - 6x + 4. To find the vertex directly, we complete the square.
Take half of b = -6, which is {{-6/2}}. Square it: {{(-6/2)^2}}. Add and subtract that inside: y = (x^2 - 6x + {{(-6/2)^2}}) + 4 - {{(-6/2)^2}}.
That first group is a perfect square, so this becomes y = (x - 3)^2 - 5, and the vertex is ({{vertexX(1,-6)}}, {{vertexY(1,-6,4)}}).
Vertex form y = a(x - h)^2 + k reveals the vertex (h, k) instantly. Here h = {{vertexX(1,-6)}} and k = {{vertexY(1,-6,4)}}.
When a > 0, k is the minimum value; when a < 0, k is the maximum. This parabola opens up, so y = {{vertexY(1,-6,4)}} is its minimum.

check.text: Complete the square for x^2 + 4x + 1. What is the vertex (h, k)?
